Hero.title: Dilnoza's English
Hero.subtitle: Elevate your language skills with a modern, AI-powered approach to learning.

Capabilities.title: What We Offer
Capabilities.chatbotDesc: Practice conversations instantly. The AI responds like Dilnoza, providing corrections and guidance in English, Russian, or Uzbek.
Capabilities.articlesDesc: Generate high-quality English articles on any topic. Perfect for practicing reading comprehension and building vocabulary.
Capabilities.libraryDesc: Browse our feed of already generated articles. Discover new topics and read what others are learning.
Capabilities.tipsDesc: Expert strategies and pro tips to help you master grammar, reading, speaking, and more.

Philosophy.title: Dilnoza's Teaching Way
Philosophy.p1: Currently pursuing her PhD, Dilnoza has dedicated her academic and professional life to understanding the most effective methods for language acquisition.
Philosophy.p2: Her methodology centers around <bold>immersive dialogue</bold> and <bold>contextual understanding</bold>. Rather than memorizing endless grammar rules, she believes students excel when they engage in meaningful conversations and read materials that genuinely interest them.
Philosophy.p3: This platform embodies her philosophy: a safe, intelligent space for students to practice actively, make mistakes, and learn naturally through cutting-edge AI.

Footer.text: © {year} Dilnoza's English. Built for educational purposes.

Library.title: AI-Generated Articles

Tips.overallSubtitle: Expert strategies for every aspect of your English learning journey.
Tips.overallD1: Studying for 15 minutes every single day is far more effective than cramming for 3 hours once a week. Language learning builds neural pathways through repeated, distributed exposure.
Tips.overallP1: Set one tiny daily goal — read one paragraph, learn 3 words — and protect it even on busy days.
Tips.overallD2: Perfectionism is the enemy of fluency. Every mistake is data: it tells your brain exactly what to reinforce next.
Tips.overallP2: Keep a small 'mistake journal'. Write down each error and the correct form. Review it weekly.
Tips.overallD3: Change your phone, browser, and social media to English. Passive exposure throughout the day adds up to hours of extra input every week.
Tips.overallP3: Switch your phone language to English for just one week and notice how quickly you start reading faster.
Tips.overallT4: Think in English, not your native language
Tips.overallD4: Translating in your head slows you down and produces unnatural phrasing. Force your brain to associate ideas directly with English words.
Tips.overallP4: Narrate your daily actions in your head: 'I am making coffee. The traffic is heavy today.'
Tips.overallD5: Apps like Anki use algorithms to show you a word exactly when you're about to forget it — the most efficient way to memorise vocabulary permanently.
Tips.overallP5: Review your Anki deck for just 10 minutes every morning before checking anything else.
Tips.readingSubtitle: Improve your comprehension, reading speed, and vocabulary through smart reading habits.
Tips.readingD1: Every well-written English paragraph has a topic sentence (usually first), supporting details, and sometimes a concluding point. Identify these and you instantly understand the skeleton of any text.
Tips.readingP1: Read only the first sentence of each paragraph to get a summary of any article in 30 seconds.
Tips.readingT2: Don't stop for every unknown word
Tips.readingD2: Stopping to look up every word destroys your reading flow and discourages you. In real life, fluent readers guess meaning from context all the time.
Tips.readingP2: Underline unknown words, finish the whole paragraph, then look them up — context will make the definition stick much better.
Tips.readingD3: If you understand 100% of what you read, it isn't helping you grow. Aim for texts where you know about 90% of the words — enough to follow along, but enough to challenge you.
Tips.readingP3: Try BBC Learning English, The Guardian, or Medium articles on topics you already love.
Tips.readingD4: Reading silently and reading aloud use different parts of the brain. Reading aloud forces you to pronounce every word and notice sentence rhythm, which improves both reading and speaking.
Tips.readingP4: Record yourself reading a paragraph aloud, then listen back. You will immediately hear pronunciation patterns to improve.
Tips.readingD5: After finishing an article, close it and write or say a 3-sentence summary in your own words. This forces active comprehension rather than passive skimming.
Tips.readingP5: Use this template: 'This article is about... The main point is... One thing I learned is...'
Tips.listeningSubtitle: Train your ear to understand natural, fast-spoken English.
Tips.listeningD1: Putting on English TV while you scroll your phone gives you almost zero benefit. True listening improvement requires your full attention on the words being said.
Tips.listeningP1: Dedicate 15 focused minutes of active listening daily rather than 3 distracted hours.
Tips.listeningD2: Shadowing means listening to a native speaker and repeating what they say almost simultaneously, matching their rhythm, speed, and intonation. It is one of the most powerful techniques ever tested.
Tips.listeningP2: Use short YouTube clips (30–60 seconds). Listen once, then shadow on the second play.
Tips.listeningD3: If you read subtitles in your native language, your brain ignores the audio entirely. Switch to English subtitles or no subtitles to force your ears to work.
Tips.listeningP3: Watch a 5-minute scene first with no subtitles, then with English subtitles to verify what you caught.
Tips.listeningT4: Listen to a variety of accents
Tips.listeningD4: English is spoken with hundreds of accents worldwide — British, American, Australian, Indian. Exposing yourself to different accents early prevents you from being caught off guard in real conversations.
Tips.listeningP4: Rotate your listening sources: one week of American podcasts, one week of British content, and so on.
Tips.listeningD5: Listen to a short clip and try to write down every word you hear. This forces you to distinguish individual words in connected speech — one of the hardest listening skills.
Tips.listeningP5: Start with slow, clear speakers (TED-Ed, BBC 6-Minute English), then move to faster natural content.
Tips.speakingSubtitle: Communicate clearly, fluently, and without fear.
Tips.speakingD1: The biggest mistake learners make is waiting until they feel 'ready' to speak. Fluency comes from speaking, not from studying. A conversation with errors beats silence every time.
Tips.speakingP1: Set a rule: answer any English question you hear (on TV, in a lesson) out loud, even if alone.
Tips.speakingT2: Describe the unknown with simpler words
Tips.speakingD2: When you forget a word mid-sentence, do not freeze. Describe what you mean using words you know. This is what native speakers do too.
Tips.speakingP2: 'I don't know the word, but it's the thing you use to... it looks like...' — keep going!
Tips.speakingD3: If you have no speaking partner, narrate your day, describe what you see, or give yourself instructions in English. This builds fluency without needing an audience.
Tips.speakingP3: Spend 5 minutes each morning describing your plans for the day out loud in English.
Tips.speakingD4: Most learners are surprised by how they actually sound. Recording yourself speaking is one of the most honest and effective forms of self-correction available.
Tips.speakingP4: Record a 1-minute voice note in English every evening. Listen back the next morning and note one thing to improve.
Tips.speakingT5: Focus on rhythm and stress, not perfection
Tips.speakingD5: English is a stress-timed language. Native speakers reduce unstressed syllables and emphasise key words. Getting this rhythm right makes you far easier to understand, even with an accent.
Tips.speakingP5: Clap the stressed words in a sentence: 'I WANT to GO to the STORE' — hear the rhythm.
Tips.writingSubtitle: Express your ideas clearly and naturally in written English.
Tips.writingT1: Write something in English every day
Tips.writingD1: A daily writing habit — even a single paragraph in a journal — builds grammar intuition faster than studying rules. Your brain starts to 'feel' when a sentence is wrong.
Tips.writingP1: Keep a private English diary. Write 5 sentences about your day every night before sleep.
Tips.writingD2: Many learners write very long, complicated sentences to sound impressive. But in English, clarity is valued far more than complexity. Short, direct sentences are a sign of skill.
Tips.writingP2: If a sentence is longer than 20 words, split it in two. Your reader will thank you.
Tips.writingD3: The best way to absorb natural sentence structure, transitions, and vocabulary is by reading extensively. You will start borrowing patterns unconsciously.
Tips.writingP3: After reading a great paragraph, copy it by hand. This 'muscle memory' method internalises its rhythm.
Tips.writingT4: Learn a set of linking words
Tips.writingD4: Words like 'however', 'therefore', 'in addition', 'as a result' are the glue of good writing. They make your ideas flow logically from one to the next.
Tips.writingP4: Choose 3 new linking words this week. Use each one at least twice in your writing.
Tips.writingD5: Good writing is rewriting. After writing a draft, wait an hour, then re-read it looking for sentences you can make shorter, words you can replace, or ideas that are unclear.
Tips.writingP5: Ask yourself: 'Could I say this in fewer words?' If yes, do it.
Tips.vocabularySubtitle: Build a rich, practical, and lasting vocabulary.
Tips.vocabularyT1: Learn words in context, not in isolation
Tips.vocabularyD1: A word you learned in a real sentence is far easier to remember than a word from a list. Your brain stores it with meaning, emotion, and context attached.
Tips.vocabularyP1: When you encounter a new word, write the whole sentence it appeared in — not just the word and its translation.
Tips.vocabularyD2: English words come in families: noun, verb, adjective, adverb. Learning all forms at once multiplies your vocabulary four times faster.
Tips.vocabularyD3: Research shows you need to use a new word at least 7 times before it transfers to long-term memory. Passive reading of a word is not enough.
Tips.vocabularyP3: After learning a word, use it in a spoken sentence, a written sentence, and a text message — all on the same day.
Tips.vocabularyT4: Learn collocations, not just single words
Tips.vocabularyD4: Native speakers don't just know words — they know which words go together. 'Make a decision' not 'do a decision'. 'Heavy rain' not 'strong rain'. These natural combinations are called collocations.
Tips.vocabularyP4: When you learn a new noun, always look up 3 verbs and 3 adjectives that commonly go with it.
Tips.vocabularyT5: Review vocabulary before you forget it
Tips.vocabularyD5: The 'forgetting curve' shows you lose 70% of new information within 24 hours unless you review it. A quick 5-minute review the next day resets the clock dramatically.
Tips.vocabularyP5: Review yesterday's vocabulary every morning before learning anything new.
Tips.grammarSubtitle: Understand grammar intuitively — without getting lost in rules.
Tips.grammarD1: You do not need all 12 English tenses to communicate. Simple Present, Present Continuous, Simple Past, Present Perfect, and Future Simple cover the vast majority of everyday conversation.
Tips.grammarP1: Write 3 original sentences using each of the 5 core tenses every day for one week. Fluency follows.
Tips.grammarD2: Instead of memorising a rule like 'the present perfect uses have/has + past participle', memorise a beautiful, natural sentence: 'I have never been to Paris.' The rule will emerge on its own.
Tips.grammarP2: For every grammar rule you learn, find 3 natural example sentences and memorise them, not the rule itself.
Tips.grammarD3: Articles are one of the hardest aspects of English for most learners because many languages don't have them. 'A dog' means any dog. 'The dog' means a specific dog we both know about. This distinction is crucial.
Tips.grammarP3: When writing, ask yourself: 'Is this specific or general?' General = a/an or no article. Specific = the.
Tips.grammarD4: Prepositions — at, in, on, for, with, by, about — are one of the trickiest parts of English because they often cannot be translated logically. They must be learned as fixed phrases.
Tips.grammarP4: Learn prepositions in fixed chunks: 'interested IN', 'good AT', 'responsible FOR', 'depend ON'.
Tips.grammarT5: Use grammar to communicate, not to show off
Tips.grammarD5: The goal of grammar is to be understood clearly. A simple sentence with correct grammar is far better than a complex sentence full of errors. Communicate first; perfect later.
Tips.grammarP5: If you're unsure about a grammar rule in a conversation, use a simpler structure you are confident about.
